role_hints.oews_basis: 23-1011 Lawyers 62.15% of wage bill (4-digit 5411 mix; for 541110 proper the lawyer share is likely even higher since siblings 541191/541199 dilute it)
role_hints.note: OEWS does not split partners from associates, and partner profit draws often sit outside QCEW wages entirely. Research, memo and brief drafting, doc review, first-draft contracts and discovery are heavily AI-exposed; court appearances, depositions, negotiation, client counseling, signing pleadings and bar-licensure liability are not. The run should split this role by seniority/task mix when setting the within-role fraction — plausibly 0.25-0.45 blended, but justify from task evidence.

Document assembly, cite-checking, discovery review, e-filing prep and docketing are directly automatable; witness/client coordination and courthouse logistics less so. One of the highest within-role fractions in the firm.

role_hints.role: Legal secretaries and office administrative staff
role_hints.oews_basis: 43-6012 Legal Secretaries 5.51% + 43-6014 Other Secretaries 0.77% + 43-9061 Office Clerks 1.00% + 43-4171 Receptionists 0.73% + 43-1011 Admin Supervisors 1.32%
role_hints.note: Scheduling, dictation/formatting, intake paperwork, filing and phone triage — near-fully automatable; this layer has been shrinking for a decade already.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 2.29% + 43-3031 Bookkeeping Clerks 0.98%
role_hints.note: Time-entry, prebill and collections workflow automates; HR, vendor and partner-compensation judgment persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: 23-2093 Title Examiners, Abstractors, and Searchers 0.82% — this occupation is 541191 contamination bleeding into the 4-digit 5411 mix; largely absent from pure law offices
role_hints.note: Treat as a sibling-code artifact; do not build a role around it for law offices.

role_hints.role: Long tail (law clerks, IT, marketing/BD, records, litigation support)
role_hints.oews_basis: not in listed top-10; est. remainder ~11% of wage bill
role_hints.note: Records, litigation-support processing and marketing content automate; business development is relationship work.

source_hints.source: ABA Legal Technology Survey Report (ABA Legal Technology Resource Center, annual)
source_hints.why: Primary lawyer-population data on AI tool adoption by firm size — directly usable for current_adoption_pct.
Utilization, realization and AI-adoption data drawn from small/mid firm practice-management telemetry — the segment that matches the $1-10M EBITDA band.

source_hints.source: Thomson Reuters Future of Professionals Report (annual)
source_hints.why: Cross-professional AI adoption and expected-impact survey covering law firms.

source_hints.source: Thomson Reuters / Georgetown Law State of the Legal Market report (annual)
source_hints.why: Rate, realization and demand-hours trends — the evidence base for whether hourly billing passes AI savings to clients.

source_hints.source: Legal Services Corporation Justice Gap Report
source_hints.why: Latent unmet consumer demand — relevant to whether volume expansion offsets price deflation at the consumer end.

source_hints.source: ABA Profile of the Legal Profession (annual)
source_hints.why: Lawyer age distribution (large 60+ cohort) for owners_60plus_pct; solo/small-firm demographics.

source_hints.source: BVR DealStats and BizBuySell Insight Report
source_hints.why: The sparse small-practice comp set (consumer practices, PI books); the thinness of arms-length law-firm comps is itself a finding.

source_hints.source: Trade coverage of MSO and Arizona ABS deals (ALM / Above the Law / Law.com reporting)
source_hints.why: The only real windows into PE structures for law — Rule 5.4 bans non-lawyer ownership except AZ ABS and UT sandbox; verify current status of both programs.

capture_questions: The dominant billing model is the hourly rate: when AI cuts the hours a matter takes, does the saving flow to the client as a smaller bill (hours-billed deflation) or can firms hold effective price via flat/value fees and higher rates? What does rate-vs-realization evidence show since 2023, and how far along is the shift away from hourly in the small-firm segment? Note contingency work (plaintiff PI) is structurally immune — fee is % of recovery.

Is the client relationship owned by the firm or portable with the individual partner — what share of small-firm revenue survives a rainmaker's exit or an acquisition, and what does that do to transferable enterprise value?

For consumer/SMB matters (wills, LLC formation, uncontested divorce, demand letters), how much volume can bypass lawyers entirely via generic AI plus e-filing before UPL enforcement bites — and are corporate clients pulling routine work in-house (GC plus AI)?
What fraction of billings rests on procedural mandates (court appearances, rights of audience, signed and attorney-certified filings) that AI cannot legally perform regardless of capability?
terminal_value_question: Licensure, rights of audience and malpractice liability mean a human lawyer must still sign and appear — but the billable-hour base underneath is what clients actually pay for. In 5-10 years, does paid demand persist at today's scale because bar rules and courts require human counsel, or does the industry compress to a stamp-appear-and-judgment layer with far fewer billed hours, with justice-gap latent demand only partially offsetting hourly-fee deflation? Distinguish AI cutting the firm's cost of production from AI shrinking the product (hours) the firm sells.
special_notes: Pricing-model deflation candidate: hourly billing converts AI efficiency directly into fewer billed hours unless firms reprice to flat/value fees — model this explicitly in pi_dist, and treat contingency (plaintiff) practices as a separate, deflation-resistant sub-segment.

Ownership restriction is the central B constraint: ABA Model Rule 5.4 (adopted in nearly every state) bans non-lawyer ownership and fee-sharing; exceptions are Arizona's ABS licenses and Utah's sandbox. PE entry elsewhere uses MSO structures whose economics and enforceability the run should verify — do not assume clean buyouts.

EBITDA normalization: small-firm 'profit' is overwhelmingly owner/partner compensation (SDE); partner draws are distributions, not QCEW wages, so both the labor_share figure and any quoted practice-sale multiples need owner-comp normalization before use.
Key-person portability: client books travel with partners; covenants-not-to-compete are ethically restricted for lawyers (Model Rule 5.6), making retention riskier than in other professions.

Segment economics diverge sharply — plaintiff contingency, insurance defense (hourly, carrier rate-capped, audited legal bills), corporate transactional, consumer flat-fee. Score the dominant small-firm mix and note heterogeneity rather than blending incompatible fee models.

Boundary confusion: 4-digit data (including the provided role_mix at 5411) blends in 541191 title/settlement and 541199 process servers/doc preparers; ABA and Clio data cover lawyers, not those siblings — check coverage before quoting.

The record's size-class data show 89% of the 156,583 firms have <10 employees and implied EBITDA far below $1M — the acquirable band (n_band 7,193) sits almost entirely in the 20-99 employee class; evidence about BigLaw does not transfer to these targets.
